payload.topic: why flaky tests erode team trust
payload.brief: sharp technical founder, no hype, numbers over adjectives

items.title: Einstein's relativity rules chemical bonds in heavy elements, new research shows
QuadRF can spot drones and see WiFi through my wall
Apple sues OpenAI, accuses ex-employees of stealing trade secrets
An update on residential proxies and the scraper situation
GPT-5.6 Sol Ultra produces proof of the Cycle Double Cover Conjecture [pdf]
SpaceX wants to launch 100k more Starlink satellites for 100x the bandwidth
The tech of 'Terminator 2' – an oral history (2017)

classify.label: opinion
classify.reasoning: The 'why...erode trust' framing argues a stance on impact rather than teaching steps or reporting news, though it borders on analysis.

rank.reasoning: Flaky tests are a near-universal, emotionally relatable pain point for engineers, giving this strong recognition and shareability, though the framing is familiar rather than novel.

write.post: Skipping a flaky test doesn't cost you one build, it costs you every green checkmark after it. Once a pass and a fail mean the same thing, people stop reading the results and start hitting rerun on reflex. Now a real failure ships, because the signal you spent months building has a known false-positive rate and everyone rounds it to zero.

issues: 'numbers over adjectives' preference not really exercised — the piece is entirely conceptual with no concrete figures, but it also makes no fake-precision claims, so this is acceptable rather than a violation
